questions.scenario: An access request says `grant Reader to Alex Chen`. The tenant has two users named Alex Chen: one is a platform engineer and one is a contractor account created for a short project. What should the reviewer ask for before approving?

options.text: The object ID or another stable identity record that identifies the exact user.
The display name again, because Entra ID prevents duplicate display names.
The user's preferred region, because region decides which user object receives access.
The resource group name only, because scope removes the need to identify the principal.

questions.explanation: Display names are human-friendly, but object IDs identify the actual principal. A reviewer needs the exact principal before granting access.

questions.scenario: A pipeline identity and a human user both have names that include `orders-deploy`. One is a service principal and one is a user account. Why does the object ID matter?

options.text: The object ID proves which principal receives the assignment, even if names look similar.
The object ID tells Azure which region the resource should run in.
The object ID replaces the need for role and scope.
The object ID is only useful for billing reports, not access decisions.

questions.explanation: Principal identity and permission are separate facts. The object ID helps identify the caller; role and scope still decide what it can do.

questions.scenario: A reviewer is checking an access request for production. Which details make the request safer to approve or reject accurately?

options.text: Principal type, such as user, group, or service principal.
Object ID for the principal.
Requested role and scope.
A screenshot of the portal search box with a display name typed in.

questions.explanation: The useful review facts identify who or what receives access and exactly what access is being requested. A search screenshot is weak evidence.

questions.scenario: A group named `Orders Admins` already exists. A new group named `Orders-Admins` is created by mistake. A role assignment is added to the wrong group. What made this failure likely?

options.text: The process trusted similar display names instead of checking the target group object ID.
Azure RBAC cannot assign roles to groups.
Groups should always be avoided because only users can be reviewed.
The subscription was in the wrong region.

questions.explanation: Groups are useful for people access, but similar names are easy to confuse. The stable object ID prevents the wrong group from being selected.

questions.scenario: A deployment pipeline uses a service principal. The team rotates the client secret but keeps the same service principal object. What stays stable for access review?

options.text: The service principal object ID and its role assignments.
The old secret value, because role assignments are attached to secrets.
The developer account that created the app registration.
The public DNS name for the service.

questions.explanation: Credentials can rotate while the service principal identity remains the same. Access review should track the principal, not a particular secret value.
